extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) holds. File: /Users/sanjaybasu/clinical-formal-verification/experiments/runs_hard/_judge_prompts_blind/item-f790f3922622.txt.

Key facts about the transition system:
1. The only transition that ever sets sent := true is t2 (on send_attempt), and its guard is "not opted_out". So sent can only become true while opted_out is false.
2. The only transition that ever writes opted_out is t1 (on optout), which sets opted_out := true; and crucially t1 atomically also sets sent := false. No transition anywhere sets opted_out := false, so opted_out is monotonic (once true, stays true).
3. Every other transition that touches sent sets it to false: t33 (send_attempt while opted_out) sets sent := false, and all key_0/key_1/key_2 transitions set sent := false.

Therefore, to reach a state with opted_out = true, the last write to opted_out must have been t1, which simultaneously cleared sent to false. After that, opted_out remains true forever, and the only way to set sent back to true is t2, which is guarded by not opted_out and thus disabled. So no reachable state can have opted_out = true and sent = true.

I confirmed this with an exhaustive breadth-first search over the complete reachable state space (state = opted_out, sent, stage). Only 33 states are reachable, and none has both opted_out and sent true. Since the full unbounded reachable set contains no violating state, the bounded requirement (executions up to 13 events) is satisfied as well. The property holds; both witness arrays are empty.
